title: Courgette and lemon loaf
subtitle: Moist quick loaf with lemon drizzle
excerpt: A moist, lemon-bright quick loaf made with grated courgette and a drizzle glaze while the loaf is still warm. The courgette keeps the crumb tender for three days without tasting of vegetables. Baking-powder leavened, mixed in one bowl, baked in under an hour.
sourceNotes: The practice of adding grated vegetables to a sweet quick loaf for moisture is documented in domestic American and British cooking manuals from the late nineteenth century onward. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the chapter on sweet breads documents the use of vegetable moisture to tenderise a baking-powder loaf, reducing the need for added fat. The lemon drizzle finish is documented in British domestic baking manuals of the early twentieth century available through the Internet Archive.

content.text: The courgette in this loaf is there for moisture, not flavour, grated and squeezed of excess water, it dissolves into the crumb during baking and keeps the loaf tender for three days. The lemon does the flavour work: zest in the batter for fragrance, juice in the drizzle for brightness. Squeeze the grated courgette as dry as you can before measuring; water left in makes the crumb gummy.

items.name: Courgette
items.prepNote: coarsely grated and squeezed dry (about 2 medium)

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: zest of both, juice of both reserved for the drizzle

content.text: Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Butter and line the loaf tin with baking paper. Grate the courgettes coarsely, then gather the gratings in a clean cloth or a few sheets of kitchen paper and squeeze firmly until no more water runs out. You want about 250 g of squeezed, dry courgette. Zest both {{lemon}}s and set the juice aside for the drizzle.

In a large bowl, whisk the {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{rapeseed-oil}} until combined. Add the squeezed {{courgette}} and the lemon zest. Sift in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Fold together until just combined, a few streaks of flour are fine; overmixing toughens the crumb.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out with just a few moist crumbs (not wet batter). The top should be cracked and firm when pressed.

While the loaf bakes, stir enough lemon juice (start with 3 tablespoons) into the sifted {{icing-sugar}} to make a pourable, slightly thick glaze. As soon as the loaf comes out of the oven, poke 10 to 12 holes in the surface with a skewer and pour the
slowly over the top. It will soak in as the loaf cools. Leave in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out.
